title: Efficient Description of Parametric Amplification of Quantum Pulses
authors: Victor Rueskov Christiansen, Klaus Mølmer, Emanuel Hubenschmid
abstract: A single quantum pulse undergoing parametric amplification feeds into at most two pulses in the output. In this work, we present an efficient, analytical method for finding the quantum state of these output modes. Our method applies the amplification to the vacuum rather than to the input state, and subsequently applies a transformed version of the operator that creates the input state from vacuum. Given the input and output pulse mode functions, the method is analytical, and therefore computationally very efficient, and it can be readily generalized to multiple non-vacuum input modes. We exemplify the method by computing the output quantum states resulting from the input of a coherent, a Schrödinger cat, and a single photon input quantum state. We further employ the method to obtain the quantum state in one of the two output modes heralded upon detection of vacuum in the other, least populated, mode.
summary: This paper introduces an efficient analytical method to calculate the output quantum state of parametric amplification when the input is a complex quantum state. By decomposing the input operator relative to the output modes, the authors simplify the calculation significantly. The method is demonstrated using various input states and highlights the utility of quantum state purification through mode heralding.
main_problem: The paper develops an efficient, analytical method to determine the quantum state of output modes following parametric amplification when the input is an arbitrary non-vacuum quantum state.
main_result: The method successfully calculates output states for various inputs (coherent, cat, single-photon) and demonstrates that vacuum detection in one output mode can significantly purify the state in the other.
method: The core method involves applying the amplification transformation to the vacuum state first, and then applying a transformed version of the operator that creates the input state from vacuum, avoiding complex ancillary mode calculations.
model_or_system: The system modeled is bosonic quantum fields (like light in waveguides) undergoing parametric amplification, described by a Hamiltonian quadratic in creation and annihilation operators.
key_observables: Purity ($\gamma$), Wigner function, covariance matrix ($\sigma$), and the quantum state itself ($\hat{ho}_{	ext{out}}$).
important_parameters: Input state parameters (e.g., $\alpha$ for coherent states), pump strength ($\xi$), and cavity loss rate ($\gamma$).
main_assumptions: The formalism relies heavily on the quadratic form of the Hamiltonian and the effective treatment of Gaussian states, although it claims generalizability.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic transformation process, the Wigner functions of input/output states, and the effect of vacuum detection on state purity.
paper_structure: The paper builds from the general mathematical formalism for state transformation through parametric amplification, details the single-mode and two-mode operator decompositions, and concludes with specific examples demonstrating state purification via heralding.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, computationally efficient analytical tool for analyzing quantum state evolution in parametric processes, which is central to quantum optics and quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic depiction of the transformation of quantum states and methods discussed in this article. An input state ˆρu in = f(ˆau, ˆa† u) |0⟩⟨0| [f(ˆau, ˆa† u)]†, generated by applying f to the vacuum, in a single pulsed mode with spectral mode function u(ω) is incident on a nonlinear system offering frequency conversion and parametric amplification. The nonlinear interaction is described by a Hamiltonian, which may be explicitly time-dependent and is quadratic in its creation/annihilation operators. Only two output modes, v1(ω) and v2(ω), will be fed by the input quantum state. The lower panels show an input mode and the corresponding state dependent output modes for an optical...
Figure 2. Wigner function of the three example input states, coherent |α⟩, cat (|α⟩+ i |−α⟩)/ √

Figure 3. The output quantum state generated from the input of either a single photon state (upper row) or an odd Schrödinger cat state (lower row) residing in the primary mode, v1(ω). The Wigner functions of the input quantum state are shown in the left column, the output quantum state in the primary v1-mode after tracing out the secondary mode in the middle column, and the quantum state of the primary mode after detection of vacuum in the secondary v2-mode in the right column. The Wigner negativity and purity of the primary mode is enhanced after the detection.
